stories.headline: JetBrains Sunsets Code With Me Collaboration Feature
stories.summary: JetBrains has announced the discontinuation of Code With Me, a collaborative coding feature integrated into their IDEs that enabled real-time pair programming and workspace sharing. The feature had been part of JetBrains development environments for years, allowing development teams to work together directly within their IDEs. This move marks the end of a significant tool for remote collaboration in the software development community.
stories.context: JetBrains makes popular computer programs that help programmers write code, similar to how Microsoft Word helps people write documents. These coding programs are called IDEs, which are like giant toolboxes containing everything a programmer needs in one place. Years ago, when more people started working from home, JetBrains added a feature called Code With Me that let programmers see and work on each other's computer screens in real time. Now JetBrains has decided to stop supporting this feature, which is like a company removing a popular tool from their toolbox, leaving programmers to find other ways to collaborate remotely.
stories.why_it_matters: A popular tool that let computer programmers work together in real-time is being shut down, making remote teamwork harder for many developers. Teams will now need to find and learn new ways to collaborate, which could slow down their work temporarily.
stories.other_side: The feature might not have been used enough to justify keeping it running, and most companies already use other collaboration tools that work just fine.

stories.headline: Major Tech Companies Pledge to Share Threat Intelligence on Scammers
stories.summary: Google, Microsoft, LinkedIn, Meta, Amazon, OpenAI, Adobe, and Match Group have signed a voluntary commitment to collaborate on sharing threat intelligence about scammers abusing their platforms. This coordinated effort aims to combat fraudulent activity across services operated by eight of the world's largest technology companies. The pledge represents a significant industry-wide initiative to address the growing problem of platform-based scams.
stories.original_title: Google, Microsoft, LinkedIn, Meta, Amazon, OpenAI, Adobe, and Match sign a voluntary pledge to share threat intelligence on scammers abusing their services (Sam Sabin/Axios)
stories.context: Scammers and criminals constantly try to trick people using technology, whether through fake emails, fraudulent profiles, or online shopping scams. These bad guys often use multiple different websites and apps to run their schemes, hopping from one platform to another when they get caught. Until now, each technology company mostly fought these scammers on their own, separate from the others. This new agreement means eight of the world's biggest technology companies have promised to share information about scammers with each other, like police departments in different cities working together to catch the same criminals.
stories.why_it_matters: Eight of the world's biggest tech companies are teaming up to share information about scammers, which could help stop more people from getting ripped off online. This cooperation across different platforms means scammers can't just hop from one service to another when caught.
stories.other_side: These promises are voluntary and have no real rules or penalties, so companies might not actually follow through when it costs them money or time.

stories.headline: Honor 600 Lite Launches with 108MP Camera and Massive Battery
stories.summary: Honor has introduced the 600 Lite smartphone featuring a 108-megapixel camera, a dedicated AI camera button, and an impressively large 6,520mAh battery within a metal build. The device is being marketed with a special Ramadan pricing strategy, aiming to offer premium features at an affordable price point. This mid-range offering highlights the competitive specifications Honor is bringing to the budget and mid-tier smartphone market.
stories.original_title: Honor 600 Lite Arrives with 108MP Camera, Massive Battery, and a Ramadan Price
stories.context: Honor is a company that makes smartphones, competing against better-known brands like Samsung and Apple. Smartphone cameras take pictures using tiny sensors that capture light, and for years phone companies have been competing to pack more megapixels into these sensors — megapixels are simply the tiny dots that make up a digital photograph, with more dots potentially meaning clearer pictures. The phone market is divided into expensive premium phones and affordable budget phones, but Honor has been trying to bring fancy features from expensive phones into cheaper ones. This new phone shows how the middle ground of the smartphone market has become incredibly competitive, with companies racing to offer cameras and batteries that were considered premium just a few years ago at prices regular people can afford.
stories.why_it_matters: A new mid-range phone is offering fancy features like an ultra-high-quality camera and huge battery at a budget-friendly price, pushing other phone makers to offer better deals. This gives regular consumers access to premium features without having to pay top dollar.
stories.other_side: High camera specs on paper don't always mean great photos in real life, and a massive battery might make the phone too heavy or bulky for some users.

stories.headline: Samsung Expands Galaxy Watch 6 Classic Update to More Regions
stories.summary: Samsung is rolling out its February 2026 firmware update for the Galaxy Watch 6 Classic to additional regions beyond Korea, with the update now reaching India and expected to expand globally. The expansion marks a broader deployment of what appears to be the latest software improvements for the previous-generation Galaxy Watch lineup. This move ensures more users can access the latest features and performance enhancements for their wearable devices.
stories.original_title: Samsung expands Galaxy Watch 6 Classic February 2026 update
stories.context: Samsung makes smartwatches that work like miniature computers you wear on your wrist, tracking your health and showing notifications from your phone. When you buy a smartwatch or any electronic device, it comes with software programmed into it, and companies regularly release updates — these are like sending the device new instructions to fix problems or add new abilities, similar to how your phone gets better over time. The "Galaxy Watch 6 Classic" is an older model from Samsung's lineup, meaning it's not the newest version they currently sell. Samsung rolling out updates to older devices in more countries is like a car company continuing to provide maintenance and improvements to a car model they stopped selling last year, showing they still care about customers who bought earlier products.
stories.why_it_matters: More people around the world can now get software updates for their Samsung watches, making their devices work better and last longer. This shows that Samsung is still supporting older products instead of forcing everyone to buy new ones.
stories.other_side: The update might not bring anything exciting or new, and some users might be frustrated it took so long to reach their country after Korea got it first.

stories.headline: Best Buy Launches Tech Fest Sale with Major Discounts
stories.original_title: Best Buy launches giant Tech Fest sale: Apple, MacBooks, 50% off TVs, FREE $50 credits, smart home, more
stories.context: Best Buy is a large retail store where people buy electronics like computers, phones, televisions, and other technology products. Stores often hold special sale events where they temporarily lower prices to attract more customers, similar to how grocery stores have weekly specials. These sales are usually given catchy names to help people remember when they're happening. When a company announces a sale event, they typically want to clear out older inventory or attract shoppers during specific times of the year.
stories.why_it_matters: A major sale event gives people the chance to buy electronics, gadgets, and tech products at lower prices, helping families save money on expensive items. Sales like this can make technology more accessible to people who otherwise couldn't afford it.
stories.other_side: Big sales sometimes trick people into buying things they don't actually need just because it seems like a good deal at the moment.

stories.headline: Warner Bros Montréal Reportedly Affected by Layoffs
stories.summary: Warner Bros Montréal has reportedly been hit with an unspecified number of staff cuts, joining the broader trend of layoffs across the gaming industry. The layoff news comes as the video game sector continues to face significant restructuring and cost-cutting measures. While the exact number of affected employees remains unclear, this development signals ongoing challenges within game development studios.
stories.original_title: Warner Bros Montréal reportedly hit with staff cuts
stories.context: Warner Bros Montréal is a video game studio that makes entertainment software, similar to how movie studios create films. The video game industry goes through cycles of good times and bad times — when the economy struggles or when games don't sell as well as expected, companies cut costs by letting employees go. Layoffs have been happening across many different industries recently, not just video games, as businesses try to save money. When a game company faces layoffs, it can affect which games get made in the future, just like how a newspaper cutting staff means fewer stories can be written.
stories.why_it_matters: Another game development studio is cutting jobs, showing that the video game industry is going through a rough patch that's hurting workers' livelihoods. These layoffs mean fewer people will be making the games that millions of people enjoy playing.
stories.other_side: Companies may need to cut costs to survive in a tough market, and smaller teams might become more efficient at making games with leaner budgets.

stories.headline: OnePlus Nord 6 Battery Champion Leaked for Early April Launch
stories.summary: The OnePlus Nord 6, expected to shake up the global mid-range smartphone market, reportedly features impressive battery capabilities and could launch as early as April. The leaked information suggests the device aims to compete aggressively in the mid-tier segment with standout battery performance. OnePlus has been positioning its Nord series to appeal to value-conscious consumers seeking premium features at lower price points.
stories.original_title: This battery champion could arrive as soon as early April, according to new leak
stories.context: OnePlus is a smartphone company that gained popularity by selling phones with excellent features at much lower prices than big brands like Samsung or Apple. They created the "Nord" line specifically as a series of mid-range phones — these are devices that cost less than the most expensive flagship phones but still offer many of their features. The smartphone market is incredibly competitive, especially in the middle price range where most regular people shop. OnePlus trying to launch a new phone with better battery life means they're trying to solve one of the most common complaints smartphone owners have: that their phone battery dies too quickly during the day.
stories.why_it_matters: A new phone with amazing battery life is coming soon at a budget price, which could force other phone companies to improve their own battery technology. This gives regular people more options for phones that won't die halfway through the day without costing a fortune.
stories.other_side: Focusing heavily on battery might mean the company cut corners on other important features like screen quality or camera performance.

stories.headline: Microsoft Criticized for Unwanted Windows 11 Features
stories.summary: A critique of Windows 11 highlights five problematic features that users describe as unnecessary add-ons nobody requested. The article outlines concerns about unfriendly design choices, aggressive monetization tactics, and overall user experience issues in Microsoft's latest operating system. This analysis reflects ongoing dissatisfaction among some users regarding Microsoft's direction with Windows 11.
stories.original_title: Microsoft has lost the plot: 5 Windows "features" nobody asked for
stories.context: Microsoft Windows is the operating system that runs on most personal computers around the world — it's the basic software that makes your computer work and lets you run other programs. Every few years, Microsoft releases a new version of Windows, and Windows 11 is their newest update that came out a few years ago, replacing the older Windows 10. Sometimes companies add new features that customers didn't ask for and don't want, often because the company is trying to make more money through advertising or selling additional services. This criticism is like restaurant customers complaining when a favorite dish gets changed with ingredients nobody requested, simply because the restaurant wants to save money or charge more.
stories.why_it_matters: Many Windows 11 users are frustrated with unwanted features that make their computers harder to use, which might push people to switch to other operating systems like Apple or Linux. This shows how adding too many bells and whistles can actually make a product worse for everyday users.
stories.other_side: Microsoft might be adding these features to make money from ads or services, which helps keep Windows free or affordable for most people.

stories.headline: DIY NAS Build Using Old Laptop Outperforms Dedicated Enclosures
stories.summary: A detailed account describes converting an older laptop into a network-attached storage system, describing three specific hardware advantages that make this approach superior to purchasing a dedicated NAS enclosure. The project offers a cost-effective alternative for tech enthusiasts looking to implement home storage solutions without spending hundreds of dollars. This DIY approach demonstrates creative problem-solving for budget-conscious home server builders.
stories.original_title: I turned an old laptop into my first NAS, and these 3 hardware perks make it better than a dedicated enclosure
stories.context: NAS stands for Network Attached Storage, which is just a fancy way of saying "a hard drive you can plug into your home internet network" so multiple computers and phones can access the same files. These devices are commonly used by people who have lots of photos, videos, or documents they want to keep safe and share across different devices. Companies sell dedicated NAS boxes that are essentially specialized computers designed just for storing files, but they can cost hundreds of dollars. This DIY approach shows how someone solved the problem creatively by using an old laptop they already owned instead of buying new equipment, similar to how someone might fix a broken appliance with spare parts rather than calling a repair service.
stories.why_it_matters: People can turn an old laptop into a home storage system that works better than buying expensive new equipment, saving money and reducing electronic waste. This clever DIY solution shows how everyday tech can be reused instead of thrown away.
stories.other_side: Taking apart and rebuilding computers requires technical skills and time that most regular people don't have, making it not practical for everyone.

stories.headline: Guide to Making Samsung Galaxy Devices Resemble Pixel Interface
stories.summary: A tutorial demonstrates how Samsung users can modify their Galaxy devices to achieve a software experience similar to Google Pixel's clean interface while retaining Samsung hardware. The guide addresses users who prefer the minimal Pixel aesthetic over Samsung's more feature-heavy One UI. This customization approach allows Galaxy owners to combine Pixel-style software design with Samsung's hardware specifications.
stories.original_title: How to make your Samsung Galaxy to look more like a Pixel device
stories.context: Google Pixel smartphones run a version of Android with a very clean, simple interface — essentially just the bare essentials without many extra features cluttering the screen. Samsung Galaxy phones also run Android, but Samsung adds its own software layer called One UI on top, which includes lots of additional features, menus, and customization options that some people find overwhelming. Some phone owners actually prefer Google's simpler look but want Samsung's hardware — meaning they like the physical phone, camera, and screen that Samsung makes, but they wish the software looked and worked differently. This guide demonstrates how tech enthusiasts can modify their phones to get the best of both worlds, similar to how someone might repaint a car to look like a different model while keeping the original engine.
stories.why_it_matters: A new guide lets Samsung phone owners make their device look and work like a Google Pixel, giving people more freedom to customize their phones however they want. This means you can have Samsung's great hardware with Google's simpler, cleaner software design.
stories.other_side: Changing your phone's software this way can be complicated and might break some features or make your phone less stable than the original software.
